parsed.title: House of Cards: Massive Weights in LLMs
parsed.field: Large Language Models / Mechanistic Analysis / Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
parsed.abstract: The paper investigates the origin of massive activations in LLMs, identifying that they originate in the intermediate state of a feed-forward network (FFN) in an early layer, not in hidden states. The authors define 'top-k massive weights' as rows of W_up and W_gate that produce the top-k magnitudes in the intermediate state. Zeroing these tiny set of weights breaks LLMs, while zeroing all other weights in the same matrices causes only minor performance drops, suggesting pre-training concentrates learning on these weights. Building on this, the authors propose MacDrop (Massive Weights Curriculum Dropout), a plug-and-play method during PEFT that applies dropout to pre-trained massive weights with a probability that decays over training. Experiments show MacDrop generally improves downstream zero-shot, long-context performance, and robustness to attacks.

sections.introduction: Motivates the investigation via prior work on attention sinks (Xiao et al., 2024) and massive activations (Sun et al., 2024a). The authors extend these analyses by (1) showing the bos token placed at the start consistently triggers massive activations, allowing simplified analysis, and (2) tracing massive activations to the intermediate state of an early-layer FFN. They define massive weights, show their disproportionate impact, and propose MacDrop for PEFT.
sections.related_work: Reviews attention sinks (Xiao et al., 2024), KV cache compression methods leveraging sinks (KVQuant, CushionCache, H2O, SnapKV), prior pre-LLM global attention work (BigBird, Longformer), the massive activation discovery (Sun et al., 2024a), and vision transformer register tokens (Darcet et al., 2024). Contrasts with Wanda/AWQ (weights identified across all layers) and concurrent 'super weight' work (Yu et al., 2024a) that examines W_down rather than W_up/W_gate.
sections.method: Defines top-k massive weights as the rows of W_up and W_gate at layer l whose output produces the top-k magnitudes in the intermediate state h_inter_l when feeding the bos token. Proposes two attacks: top-k zeroing (zero out massive weights) and top-k retaining (zero everything except massive weights). Proposes MacDrop: at each fine-tuning step, apply element-wise dropout to the pre-trained massive weight rows with probability p_t = p_0 * (1 - t/T), restoring original values after the step. Designed to reduce reliance on massive weights early and converge to using untouched pre-trained weights at the end.
sections.experiments: Experiments cover: (1) tracing massive activations through layers in Llama-2/3/3.1, Mistral, Mixtral, Phi-3, Gemma-2 families; (2) impact of top-k zeroing vs retaining on perplexity (WikiText, C4, PG-19) and zero-shot tasks (ARC-E/C, BoolQ, PIQA, WinoGrande); (3) PEFT with LoRA and DoRA on alpaca_gpt4_en for Llama-3-8B and Mistral-7B with/without MacDrop, evaluated on zero-shot tasks; (4) LongBench long-context evaluation; (5) robustness under top-3 zeroing attacks; (6) ablations on dropout scope, dropout probability, and curriculum schedules.
sections.results: Top-5 zeroing drastically degrades Llama-3-8B/70B/405B (e.g., perplexity 8.22 -> 122.74 for 8B; zero-shot 73.0 -> 39.4). Top-5 retaining (keeping only ~0.0005% of params in those matrices) suffers far less (8.22 -> 20.95; 73.0 -> 71.0). MacDrop improves zero-shot averages modestly (~0.4-0.9 points) and consistently across LoRA/DoRA on Llama-3-8B and Mistral-7B. It substantially improves robustness under top-3 zeroing attacks (e.g., Llama-3-8B+DoRA: 40.3 -> 72.7). LongBench averages improve (e.g., LoRA Llama-3-8B 39.03 -> 40.54). Gemma-2 and Phi-3-medium are insensitive to massive weight attacks and MacDrop yields no gains for them. Generation task experiments (Spec-Bench) show negligible improvements.
sections.limitations: MacDrop is not effective for models that don't exhibit strong massive phenomena (Gemma-2, Phi-3-medium). Generation task improvements are limited. The analysis is restricted to bos-triggered massive activations.
sections.conclusion: Identifies massive weights in an early-layer FFN as a small but critical set predominantly learned during pre-training. Proposes MacDrop to mitigate over-reliance on these weights during PEFT, demonstrating gains in performance and robustness for affected LLMs. Suggests further study of LLM weight space.

main_claims: Massive activations originate in the intermediate state of an early FFN layer and propagate through residual connections.
The bos token placed at the starting position consistently triggers massive activations across LLM families, enabling simplified analysis.

A tiny set of 'massive weights' (rows of W_up and W_gate at one specific layer) dominates LLM functionality: zeroing them breaks the model while zeroing all other weights in the same matrices causes relatively small degradation.
MacDrop, applying decaying dropout to pre-trained massive weights during PEFT, improves zero-shot and long-context performance and substantially improves robustness to massive-weight zeroing attacks.
Models without massive activation phenomena (Gemma-2 family, Phi-3-medium) are robust to massive weight attacks and do not benefit from MacDrop.
key_contributions: Identification that massive activations originate in the FFN intermediate state of an early layer rather than the hidden state.
Definition of top-k massive weights (rows of W_up/W_gate producing top-k intermediate state magnitudes) and characterization of their outsized impact via top-k zeroing and top-k retaining attacks.
Empirical study across many LLM families (Llama-2/3/3.1, Mistral, Mixtral, Phi-3, Gemma-2) showing varied sensitivity to massive weight attacks.
MacDrop algorithm: a plug-and-play curriculum dropout on pre-trained massive weights during PEFT (LoRA/DoRA).
Ablation studies on dropout scope, probability, and curriculum schedule.
experimental_results: Llama-3-8B: top-5 zeroing collapses zero-shot average from 73.0 to 39.4 and increases perplexity from 8.22 to 122.74; top-5 retaining only drops to 71.0 (perplexity 20.95).
Llama-3-70B and 3.1-405B show similar dramatic collapse under top-5 zeroing (perplexity reaches thousands).
Sensitivity to k varies by family: Llama is highly sensitive; Mistral collapses at k=5; Mixtral needs ~top-50 zeroing for noticeable damage; Phi-3-medium and Gemma-2 are robust.
Mixtral routing becomes nearly deterministic to a single expert (expert 4) at the layer with massive weights.
MacDrop with LoRA on Llama-3-8B: zero-shot avg 76.0 -> 76.5; with DoRA 76.1 -> 76.4; similar small gains on Mistral-7B.
Robustness: under top-3 zeroing post-fine-tuning, Llama-3-8B+DoRA drops from 76.1 to 40.3 without MacDrop but only 76.4 to 72.7 with MacDrop; Mistral-7B+DoRA: 75.3->56.4 vs 76.1->73.3.
LongBench: MacDrop improves average scores (e.g., Llama-3-8B LoRA 39.03 -> 40.54; Mistral-7B LoRA 36.91 -> 38.03).
Ablation: dropping only massive weights with moderate p outperforms dropout on all weights or non-massive weights; step-wise or exponential curriculum from moderately high p_0 works best.
Generation tasks (Spec-Bench, Prometheus-2 scoring) show negligible improvements with MacDrop.
assumptions_and_limitations: Analysis relies on using the bos token as a probe; may not capture all conditions triggering massive activations.
Massive weights are defined per a single early layer determined empirically per model.
MacDrop benefits only models exhibiting strong massive-activation phenomena (does not help Gemma-2, Phi-3-medium).
Improvements on standard benchmarks (zero-shot, generation) are small in absolute terms; the strongest effect is robustness under adversarial zeroing, which is a self-defined attack.
Experiments largely limited to Llama-3-8B and Mistral-7B for MacDrop's main evaluation; 70B/405B not evaluated for MacDrop fine-tuning.
Llama-3.1-405B experiments use an 8-bit quantized model due to GPU constraints.

Figure 2: Mistral-7B hidden state magnitudes and attention scores depending on bos token position.
Figure 3: Magnitudes across states/layers showing massive activations originate in FFN intermediate state at layer 2 of Llama-3-8B.
Figure 4: Zero-shot accuracy vs k for top-k zeroing and top-k retaining across many LLMs.
Figure 5: Mixtral-8x7B expert routing distribution showing one expert dominates at the massive-weight layer.

Figure 7: Visualization of curriculum schedules (Step, Epoch-before/after, Exponential).
Table 1: Perplexity and zero-shot accuracy under top-5 zeroing vs retaining for Llama-3-8B/70B/405B.
Table 2: LongBench results showing MacDrop gains for Llama-3-8B and Mistral-7B.

equations_algorithms: Eq. (1): Standard transformer layer with pre-norm: h_l = ĥ_l + FFN(LN(ĥ_l)), ĥ_l = h_{l-1} + ATTN(LN(h_{l-1})).
Eq. (2): Phi-3 variant with dropout on attention and FFN outputs in residual stream.
Eq. (3): Gemma-2 variant with extra LN after attention and FFN modules.
FFN decomposition: W_down(σ(W_gate(·)) ⊙ W_up(·)), with intermediate state defined as σ(W_gate(·)) ⊙ W_up(·).

Algorithm 1 (MacDrop): identify top-k massive weight rows in W_up/W_gate via bos token's intermediate state; per training step, apply Bernoulli mask with probability p_t = p_0 * (1 - t/T), then rollback to original values; orthogonal to adapter training.

parsed.field_and_subfield: Large Language Models — mechanistic interpretability and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)
parsed.problem_addressed: Understanding the origin of massive activations in LLMs at the weight level, and leveraging this understanding to improve fine-tuning robustness and performance.
parsed.research_question: Where in the network do massive activations originate, which weights are responsible for them, how critical are these weights to overall LLM functionality, and can dropout-based regularization on these weights during PEFT improve downstream performance and robustness?
parsed.one_paragraph_summary: The paper extends prior work on massive activations and attention sinks by showing that (i) the bos token at position 0 reliably triggers massive activations across most LLM families, enabling a simplified probe; and (ii) massive activations originate in the intermediate state of an early-layer feed-forward network (FFN) and propagate through residual connections. The authors define 'top-k massive weights' as the rows of W_up and W_gate at that specific layer whose outputs produce the top-k magnitudes in the intermediate state. They demonstrate via two complementary attacks (top-k zeroing vs. top-k retaining) that this tiny set (~0.0005% of parameters in Llama-3-8B) dominates model functionality: zeroing them collapses perplexity and zero-shot accuracy, while zeroing everything except them in the same matrices preserves most performance. Building on this, they propose MacDrop, a curriculum dropout applied to pre-trained massive weight rows during LoRA/DoRA fine-tuning with linearly (or exponentially) decaying probability. MacDrop yields modest improvements on zero-shot tasks and LongBench, and substantial robustness improvements against the proposed zeroing attacks, but is ineffective for models that do not exhibit strong massive-activation phenomena (Gemma-2, Phi-3-medium).
parsed.problem_statement: Despite recent discoveries of massive activations and attention sinks, prior work has only characterized these as activation-level phenomena. It remains unclear which specific weights produce these activations, how concentrated learning is in those weights, and whether this knowledge can be exploited to improve PEFT.
main_contributions: Empirical localization of massive activations to the intermediate state of an early FFN layer (rather than the hidden state) and to a specific layer l per model.
Definition of 'top-k massive weights' as the rows of W_up and W_gate at layer l producing the top-k magnitudes of the intermediate state when fed the bos token.
Demonstration via two opposing attacks (top-k zeroing vs. top-k retaining) that these ~0.0005% parameters dominate LLM behavior in Llama-2/3/3.1, Mistral, Mixtral, and Phi-3-mini families.

Cross-family characterization showing Gemma-2 (with extra LN) and Phi-3-medium (with dropout in residuals) are robust to such attacks, suggesting architectural choices can mitigate over-reliance on massive weights.
MacDrop: a plug-and-play curriculum dropout method applied to pre-trained massive weights during PEFT, with ablations on scope, probability, and schedule.
Evidence that MacDrop improves zero-shot accuracy modestly, LongBench performance, and especially robustness to top-k zeroing attacks on fine-tuned models.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Massive activations originate in the intermediate state of an FFN module in an early layer, not in the hidden state.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Figure 3(a,c) traces magnitudes through layer-wise states in Llama-3-8B and shows FFN(LN(ĥ_2)) exhibits massive activations before h_2 does; appendix D extends this to many model families.

claim_evidence_map.claim: The bos token at the starting position consistently triggers massive activations across LLM families, enabling a unified analysis.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Figure 2 (Mistral-7B), Appendix C with bos-position experiments across Llama-2/3, Mistral/Mixtral, Phi-3, Gemma-2 families.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Top-k massive weights are an extremely small fraction of parameters yet dominate functionality.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 1: top-5 zeroing collapses Llama-3-8B perplexity from 8.22 to 122.74 and zero-shot avg from 73.0 to 39.4; top-5 retaining (zeroing ~14k rows except 5) drops only to 71.0. Massive weights are ~0.0005% of total parameters.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Sensitivity to massive weight attacks varies across architectures.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Figure 4 shows Llama and Mistral families collapse under small k; Phi-3-medium more robust; Gemma-2 essentially unaffected even when W_up/W_gate at layer l are fully zeroed.

claim_evidence_map.claim: In Mixtral, routing is nearly deterministic to one expert at the massive-weight layer.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Figure 5 visualizes expert probability distribution, showing expert 4 dominates at layer 2.
Table 3: Llama-3-8B LoRA avg 76.0->76.5, DoRA 76.1->76.4; Mistral-7B LoRA 75.3->76.2.

claim_evidence_map.claim: MacDrop substantially improves robustness under top-3 zeroing attacks.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 4: Llama-3-8B+DoRA avg drops from 76.1 to 40.3 without MacDrop under attack, but only from 76.4 to 72.7 with MacDrop; Mistral-7B+DoRA: 56.4 vs. 73.3.

claim_evidence_map.claim: MacDrop improves long-context performance on LongBench.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 2: Llama-3-8B LoRA avg 39.03->40.54; DoRA 39.07->39.48; Mistral-7B LoRA 36.91->38.03.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Ablations indicate dropout should target only massive weights with a moderately high initial probability and a step/exp curriculum.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Figure 6 (dropout scope/probability) and Table 5 (curriculum and p_0).

claim_evidence_map.claim: MacDrop offers no gains for models without massive-activation phenomena.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Appendix F Table 8: Gemma-2 family and Phi-3-medium show no improvement or slight degradation with MacDrop.

sections.name: Abstract
sections.summary: Summarizes the key findings: massive activations originate in the FFN intermediate state rather than hidden states; defines massive weights and contrasts their dramatic impact with the small fraction of parameters they constitute; introduces MacDrop as a PEFT method applying decaying dropout to massive weights.
key_claims: Massive activations originate in FFN intermediate state, not hidden state.
Zeroing top-k massive weights destroys LLM functionality.
Zeroing all other weights (much larger set) has relatively minor effect.
MacDrop improves performance and robustness via decaying dropout on massive weights.
supporting_evidence: References to subsequent sections and experiments (zero-shot tasks, long-context, ablations).
issues: Claim that learning is 'dominantly focused' on massive weights is an inferential leap from the attack results in the abstract.
'Generally improves performance' is vague and does not flag the negligible/absent gains for Gemma-2 and generation tasks.
missing_information: No quantitative summary of improvement magnitudes.
No mention of model families/sizes where MacDrop fails.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: The causal claim that pretraining is dominantly focused on massive weights is not directly demonstrated (only post-hoc attack evidence is shown).

sections.name: Introduction
sections.summary: Frames work via attention sinks (Xiao et al., 2024) and massive activations (Sun et al., 2024a). Highlights two new observations (bos token always triggers massive activations; activations originate in FFN intermediate state) and previews the MacDrop method.
key_claims: Use of bos token enables simplified analysis on feature dimensions only.
Massive activations originate in intermediate state of an early FFN.
Massive weights occupy a tiny fraction (~0.0005% in Llama-3-8B).
MacDrop reduces dependence on massive weights during fine-tuning.
supporting_evidence: References to Figure 1 with schematic and generation examples.
Citations to prior attention sinks and massive activations work.
issues: The introduction states bos token 'always' triggers massive activations across LLMs, but the body shows this is not universal (e.g., Gemma-2-27b only triggers when bos is at the first position; some models respond to position rather than bos).
Statement that 'massive weights are dominantly learned during pre-training' is an interpretation; the evidence only shows their post-hoc importance, not learning dynamics.
missing_information: No clear formal definition of 'massive activations' threshold is given in the intro.
Does not specify how layer l is determined.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: 'Implies that the feed-forward network in layer l plays a crucial role in LLMs' is broadly stated without precise scope.
Causal language ('learning is dominantly focused on massive weights') goes beyond what observational attacks can establish.

sections.name: Related Work (Appendix H)
sections.summary: Surveys attention sinks, KV cache compression, prior global attention concepts, massive activations, vision register tokens, and prunning/quantization methods (Wanda, AWQ). Distinguishes from concurrent 'super weight' work.
key_claims: Massive weights are confined to one specific layer, unlike Wanda/AWQ that operate per linear layer.
Yu et al. (2024a)'s super weight is defined via W_down and considers both intermediate and hidden states, while this work focuses on W_up rows from intermediate-state output perspective.
supporting_evidence: Citations to multiple prior works on KV cache compression, sink tokens, and pruning.
issues: Does not directly compare empirical results to Wanda/AWQ or super weight work.
No quantitative differentiation from Yu et al. (2024a) is presented (e.g., overlap of identified weights, performance comparison).
missing_information: Missing discussion of prior dropout-during-fine-tuning methods (e.g., DropConnect, LoRA dropout variants, structured masking).
No discussion of weight-importance-based regularization literature relevant to MacDrop's design.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim that this work 'focuses more deeply on a narrowly defined aspect' compared to Wanda/AWQ is qualitative.

sections.name: Method (Section 2 - Massive Weights)
sections.summary: Reviews prior background, presents the bos-token observation, traces massive activations through layer states to identify FFN intermediate state as origin, and formally defines top-k massive weights as rows of W_up and W_gate at a specific layer l whose outputs yield top-k magnitudes in h_inter_l.
key_claims: Using bos token alone suffices to identify massive activations consistently.
Layer l is determined empirically per model based on where massive activations first appear.
Top-k massive weights = rows of W_up and W_gate at layer l corresponding to top-k magnitudes in h_inter_l.
supporting_evidence: Figures 2, 3 showing activation magnitudes by position and across layers.
Appendix B listing identified layers and indices for each model.
Appendix C/D extending analysis across many LLM families.
issues: No formal/quantitative threshold for 'massive' is given; identification is qualitative based on top-k by magnitude.
Selection of layer l is post-hoc inspection without an algorithmic criterion.
Definition uses bos token's intermediate state only, which may not generalize to typical model usage with content tokens.
FFN decomposition formula W_down(σ(W_gate(·)) ⊙ W_up(·)) is presented but it's unclear which σ applies (SiLU in Llama; the paper does not specify).

Mixtral analysis introduces ad hoc selection of single expert (expert 4); the bos token effectively triggers near-deterministic routing, but this may not represent the model's behavior on real inputs.
missing_information: No analysis of whether massive weight indices remain stable across different probe tokens or inputs.
No discussion of how massive weights relate to specific bos token's embedding (i.e., is the relationship effectively a memorized lookup?).
No statistical robustness test (e.g., variance of intermediate-state magnitudes).
ambiguous_or_unsupported: 'Massive activations originate in the intermediate state of a FFN module in an early layer' — strictly, the observation is empirical and limited; the causal chain through residual connections is asserted but not proved.
Claim that massive weights are 'predominantly learned during pre-training' is unsupported by training-dynamics evidence.

sections.name: Method (Section 3 - MacDrop)
sections.summary: Proposes MacDrop: identify massive weight rows via bos token; at each training step apply Bernoulli dropout mask with decaying probability p_t = p_0 * (1 - t/T) to those rows, train as usual, then rollback masked weights to originals. Orthogonal to adapter training.
key_claims: Applying dropout to weights (vs activations) is preferred because of larger element count (k×d).
Initial high dropout reduces reliance on massive weights; decaying schedule ensures convergence with intact pretrained weights.
Overhead is negligible (~0.35 s/step for Llama-3-8B with LoRA on 8xA100).
supporting_evidence: Ablation results in Section 4.3 motivating curriculum and scope choices.
issues: Algorithm 1 applies dropout at each training step but rolls back after each step — the adapter's gradient depends on masked weights, but since W is frozen, the only effect is on the forward signal seen by the adapter. This mechanism should be clarified more explicitly.
Theoretical justification for why reducing reliance on massive weights via dropout should improve generalization is heuristic.
Curriculum schedule is linear by default; choice of T (total steps) and interaction with PEFT training dynamics not discussed.
Algorithm uses non-scaled dropout (mask×W rather than mask×W/(1-p)), which causes a systematic downward shift of forward activations through these rows; this is not discussed.
missing_information: No discussion of how MacDrop interacts with full fine-tuning vs PEFT specifically.
No analysis of whether MacDrop changes the position/identity of massive weights post fine-tuning.
No control comparing dropout on random rows of same count to isolate massive-weight specificity (this is partially addressed in ablation but only via scope, not random subsets).
ambiguous_or_unsupported: 'Excessive dominance may lead the model to over-rely on specific patterns' is an intuitive claim without supporting analysis (e.g., information-theoretic or training-loss evidence).

sections.name: Experiments (Section 4)
sections.summary: Evaluates: zero-shot downstream tasks with LoRA/DoRA ± MacDrop on Llama-3-8B and Mistral-7B; robustness under top-3 zeroing post fine-tuning; LongBench long-context; ablations on dropout scope, probability, curriculum.
key_claims: MacDrop yields modest but consistent zero-shot gains.
MacDrop substantially improves robustness to massive-weight zeroing attacks.
Step or exponential curriculum from moderately high p_0 (~0.8) is recommended.
supporting_evidence: Figures 6 and 7 and Table 5 for ablations.
Appendix F extending zero-shot results to more LLMs.
issues: Zero-shot gains are very small (often <1 point average), within plausible variance; no error bars, seeds, or significance tests reported.
Robustness evaluation uses the same attack family proposed by the authors (top-3 zeroing) — somewhat circular as MacDrop is explicitly designed to reduce reliance on these weights.
Main MacDrop experiments use only two models (Llama-3-8B, Mistral-7B) and one fine-tuning dataset (alpaca_gpt4_en, 3 epochs).
Appendix F shows neutral/negative results on Gemma-2 and Phi-3-medium and minor noise on Llama-2-13B, indicating limited generality.
Generation task results (Appendix G) show essentially no improvement, contradicting the abstract's strong framing.
LongBench evaluation lacks comparison to baselines other than vanilla LoRA/DoRA; no per-task variance.
Mixtral attack uses only expert 4's weights; results may not reflect realistic deployment where multiple experts are activated.
Llama-3.1-405B uses 8-bit quantization, which may bias activation magnitudes and attack sensitivity.
missing_information: No reporting of variance across seeds.
No comparison to relevant baselines (e.g., applying random-row dropout, LoRA dropout, weight decay on massive rows).
No analysis of whether massive weights remain massive after fine-tuning with/without MacDrop.
No hyperparameter sensitivity study for k.
No comparison with concurrent super weight method (Yu et al., 2024a).
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim 'MacDrop generally improves performance' is undermined by the appendix tables showing flat/negative results for several models.
Robustness gains depend on the specific attack defined by the authors; generalization to other adversarial settings is not demonstrated.

sections.name: Limitations / Impact Statement
sections.summary: Brief impact statement notes potential safety risk of disclosed model weights and frames MacDrop as a robustness mitigation. Limitations are not consolidated into a dedicated section.
key_claims: Massive weights are not inherently risky if weights are private.
Model developers should ensure robustness.
supporting_evidence: Discussion in Section 2 of models that are not sensitive (Gemma-2, Phi-3-medium).
issues: No explicit limitations section; limitations are scattered across appendices.
Does not discuss limits of bos-only probing, post-hoc layer selection, or applicability beyond the tested PEFT setup.
Threat-model framing for the 'attack' is unclear: a party with full weight access can do many destructive things; the practical risk of this specific attack is not elaborated.
missing_information: No discussion of compute/scaling limitations (e.g., why only 8B and 7B models were fine-tuned with MacDrop).
No analysis of failure modes or negative results in main text.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: The framing of robustness improvement as 'meaningful impact' overstates the practical value of mitigating a self-defined white-box attack.

sections.name: Conclusion
sections.summary: Reiterates the discovery of massive weights and proposes MacDrop. Encourages further weight-space research.
key_claims: Massive weights are dominantly pre-trained and critical to performance.
MacDrop is an effective plug-and-play PEFT augmentation.
supporting_evidence: Refers back to Sections 2 and 4 results.
issues: Reasserts strong claims without nuancing model-family limitations or small effect sizes.
missing_information: No directions for resolving the negative results on Gemma-2/Phi-3-medium.
No discussion of theoretical understanding of why these weights emerge.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: 'Predominantly pre-trained' remains an interpretation without dynamic learning evidence.

sections.name: Supplementary (Appendices A-H)
sections.summary: Provides implementation details, identified massive-weight indices, extensive bos-token analyses across LLM families, intermediate-state visualizations, attention-sink visualizations, broader zero-shot results, generation task evaluations, and related work.
key_claims: Across many LLM families, massive activations and intermediate-state spikes are consistent at specific early layers.
Gemma-2 exhibits qualitatively different dynamics (gradual hidden-state growth and absence of attention sinks for non-bos contexts).
MacDrop fails to help models without massive phenomena.
Generation tasks show negligible MacDrop improvements.
supporting_evidence: Figures 8-24 showing per-model magnitudes and attention.
Tables 7-10 listing indices, broader benchmark results, and generation examples.
issues: Appendix C shows contradictory behavior to the main text's claim that bos always triggers massive activations (e.g., Llama-2 and Phi-3 do not respond to bos in middle/end positions).
Appendix G's generation-task null results are buried and not reflected in main-text framing.
Massive-weight indices in Table 7 differ across model versions but no discussion of stability or interpretation.
Prometheus-2-7B is used as an evaluator without discussion of its limitations or alternative metrics.
missing_information: No ablation isolating effect of k on MacDrop performance.
No long-context evaluation under attack.
No analysis of why specific feature dimensions/indices are selected by pretraining.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Interpretation that Phi-3-medium's robustness stems from dropout during pre-training is speculative (technical report does not specify recipe).
Claim that Gemma-2's extra LN layers explain its insensitivity is correlational, not causally tested.

claims.claim: Massive activations originate in the intermediate state of an FFN module in an early layer, not in the hidden state.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 3(a,c) traces magnitudes through layer-wise states in Llama-3-8B; Appendix D shows top-3 and median magnitudes of hidden and intermediate states across many model families (Llama-2/3, Mistral/Mixtral, Phi-3, Gemma-2).
claims.notes: Visual evidence is consistent and replicated across families. The localization is empirical rather than mechanistic, but the figures clearly support the temporal/spatial origin claim.

claims.claim: The bos token at the starting position consistently triggers massive activations across LLM families, enabling a unified, simplified analysis.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 2 shows bos-position effects on Mistral-7B; Appendix C visualizations cover Llama-2/3, Mistral/Mixtral, Phi-3, and Gemma-2 families with varying bos positions.
claims.notes: Notable exception: Gemma-2-27b does not trigger massive activations from bos in non-first positions (Appendix C.5). Claim is supported with this caveat but is slightly broadened in the abstract.

claims.claim: Top-k massive weights constitute ~0.0005% of parameters in Llama-3-8B yet dominate model functionality.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 1: top-5 zeroing collapses Llama-3-8B perplexity from 8.22 to 122.74 and zero-shot avg from 73.0 to 39.4; top-5 retaining drops only to 71.0. Parameter ratio computed from 2*k*4096 vs. total parameters.
claims.notes: The contrast between zeroing and retaining is striking and supports the dominance claim. The framing as 'house of cards' is somewhat sensational but evidence is clear.

claims.claim: Sensitivity to massive weight attacks varies across architectures; Gemma-2 and Phi-3-medium are robust.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 4 (zeroing/retaining vs. k) across 12 LLMs; discussion attributing Gemma-2 robustness to extra LN layers (Eq. 3) and Phi-3-medium robustness to residual dropout (Eq. 2).
claims.notes: The architectural attribution is plausible but not causally verified (no controlled ablation training a Llama-like model with extra LN or dropout).

claims.claim: In Mixtral, routing is nearly deterministic to one expert at the massive-weight layer.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 5 shows expert 4 receiving near-unit probability at layer 2 for the bos token.
claims.notes: Single-token analysis. Generalization across tokens or sequences not shown but the bos-specific claim is supported.

claims.claim: MacDrop generally improves zero-shot downstream performance during PEFT.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 3 shows small gains: Llama-3-8B LoRA 76.0->76.5, DoRA 76.1->76.4; Mistral-7B LoRA 75.3->76.2; Appendix F Table 8 extends to more models.
claims.notes: Gains are small (0.4-0.9 points) with no error bars / seeds reported. Several models (Gemma-2, Phi-3-medium, Llama-3-70B) show negligible or negative effects. Statistical significance is not established.

claims.claim: MacDrop substantially improves robustness under top-3 zeroing attacks.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 4: Llama-3-8B+DoRA drops from 76.1->40.3 without MacDrop under attack but only 76.4->72.7 with MacDrop; Mistral-7B+DoRA: 75.3->56.4 vs. 76.1->73.3.
claims.notes: Effect is large and consistent across the two models tested. However, this is robustness against the authors' own attack, which is somewhat circular as a motivation. Robustness to other attack types is not tested.

claims.claim: MacDrop improves long-context performance on LongBench.
claims.notes: Gains are mixed across subtasks (some large gains, some regressions per-task). Aggregate gains are modest, single-seed, no significance testing.

claims.claim: Ablations indicate dropout should target only massive weights with moderately high initial probability and a step/exp curriculum.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 6 shows mean zero-shot accuracy by dropout scope/probability; Table 5 compares curricula and initial probabilities.
claims.notes: Differences in Table 5 are within ~0.5 points; given likely seed noise, recommendations may be over-specific. Only Llama-3-8B is ablated.

claims.claim: MacDrop offers no gains for models without massive-activation phenomena.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Appendix F Table 8 shows Gemma-2 family and Phi-3-medium with neutral or slightly negative MacDrop effects.
Appendix G Table 9: average Prometheus scores essentially flat (3.82 vs 3.81 for LoRA Llama-3-8B; 3.80 vs 3.85 DoRA).
claims.notes: Authors acknowledge limited generation gains, which appropriately calibrates the abstract's broader claims.

claims.claim: Massive weights are 'predominantly learned during pre-training'.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Inferred from the asymmetry between top-k zeroing vs. retaining attacks, plus observation that pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants have identical massive-weight indices (Appendix B).
claims.notes: This is an interpretive leap: dominance under attack does not directly establish what was 'learned'. No training-dynamics analysis (e.g., gradient or weight-trajectory during pre-training) is provided. The claim is more of a hypothesis.

claims.claim: Pre-training dataset does not significantly influence the emergence of massive phenomena.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Inferred from observation that different Llama versions (different training data/cutoffs) exhibit similar tendencies under attack (Figure 4).
claims.notes: Confounds remain: Llama versions share much of the architecture and training recipe. No controlled study varies only data.

claims.claim: Massive weights are distinct from / a narrower instance of weights identified by Wanda and AWQ.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Appendix H argues massive weights would be among those selected by Wanda/AWQ but are confined to a single specific layer.
claims.notes: No empirical overlap analysis is provided to substantiate this relationship.

unsupported_claims: Massive weights are 'dominantly learned during pre-training' — supported only indirectly via attack asymmetry; no training-dynamics evidence.
Pre-training dataset does not significantly influence emergence of massive phenomena — based on weak cross-version comparison with multiple confounds.
Architectural choices (extra LN in Gemma-2, dropout in Phi-3-medium) cause robustness to attacks — plausible but not causally tested via controlled training experiments.
overstated_claims: Abstract: 'MacDrop generally improves performance' — gains in zero-shot and long-context tasks are small (often <1 point), single-seed, with no significance testing; effective only for a subset of models.
'House of cards' framing suggesting LLMs can be easily 'attacked' — this assumes white-box access to weights and the ability to modify them, which is a non-standard threat model.
Recommendation of specific curriculum hyperparameters based on ablations where differences are within plausible seed noise (Table 5 spans 75.5-76.5).
claims_requiring_external_verification: Whether the top-k massive weight rows substantially overlap with weights selected by Wanda/AWQ (claimed but not measured).
Whether the architectural differences (extra LN, residual dropout) causally explain robustness — would require controlled re-training.
Whether MacDrop's robustness gains generalize to attacks other than the authors' own top-k zeroing (e.g., random weight perturbations, quantization noise).
Statistical significance of MacDrop improvements across multiple seeds.
Whether massive weights identified via bos token coincide with those identified using diverse calibration inputs.

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: Empirical finding that massive activations originate in the FFN intermediate state (output of σ(W_gate·)⊙W_up·) of an early layer, before appearing in the residual stream, and propagate forward via skip connections.
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: This localization is the foundational observation that motivates the rest of the paper (massive weights, MacDrop). Its novelty depends on whether Sun et al. 2024a or follow-ups already traced this origin.
search_keywords: intermediate state LLM outlier early layer
novelty_axes.search_question: Did Sun et al. 2024a or subsequent work already attribute massive activations to the FFN intermediate state rather than the hidden state?
novelty_axes.closest_prior_work_ref: Sun et al. 2024a [similar_prior_work #1]; Yu et al. 2024a [similar_prior_work #2]

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: Definition of 'top-k massive weights' as the rows of W_up and W_gate at a specific early layer whose product produces the top-k magnitudes of the intermediate state under a bos token, and two diagnostic attacks (top-k zeroing vs top-k retaining).
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: This is the central conceptual contribution moving from activation-level to weight-level analysis. Its novelty hinges on concurrent 'super weight' work by Yu et al. 2024a.
search_keywords: critical weights FFN gate up projection
novelty_axes.search_question: Has prior or concurrent work defined a small set of critical weights in LLMs whose ablation disproportionately destroys model behavior?
novelty_axes.closest_prior_work_ref: Yu et al. 2024a [similar_prior_work #2]; Sun et al. 2024b (Wanda) [similar_prior_work #3]; Lin et al. 2024 (AWQ) [similar_prior_work #4]

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: MacDrop: a curriculum weight-dropout schedule that, during PEFT (LoRA/DoRA), drops out only the pre-trained massive-weight rows with a probability that linearly/exponentially decays to zero, leaving the original pre-trained weights intact at convergence.
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: MacDrop is the only explicit algorithmic proposal of the paper; novelty depends on whether targeted weight-level curriculum dropout for PEFT has been studied.
novelty_axes.search_question: Has any prior work used curriculum-scheduled DropConnect-style dropout applied selectively to identified critical pre-trained weights during parameter-efficient fine-tuning?
novelty_axes.closest_prior_work_ref: Wan et al. 2013 (DropConnect) [similar_prior_work #5]; Morerio et al. 2017 (Curriculum Dropout) [similar_prior_work #6]

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: Cross-family empirical taxonomy of robustness to massive-weight attacks: Llama-2/3 highly sensitive, Mistral sensitive, Mixtral routes deterministically to a single expert at the massive-weight layer, Phi-3-medium and Gemma-2 robust — and attribution to architectural differences (residual dropout, extra LN layers).
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: Provides a broad and apparently new survey of which architectures exhibit the phenomenon, with a Mixtral MoE-routing observation not (to my knowledge) previously published.
novelty_axes.search_question: Has the cross-family sensitivity analysis (especially Mixtral router collapse and Gemma-2 absence of sinks) been documented before?
novelty_axes.closest_prior_work_ref: Sun et al. 2024a [similar_prior_work #1] covers Llama/Mistral/GPT-2; Gemma-2 and 405B/MoE coverage here appears new

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: Empirical demonstration that fine-tuning with MacDrop substantially improves robustness of fine-tuned models to top-k zeroing attacks (e.g., Llama-3-8B+DoRA drops from 76.4→72.7 under attack with MacDrop vs 76.1→40.3 without).
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: This is MacDrop's strongest empirical effect; novelty depends on whether prior 'super-weight-aware fine-tuning' methods exist.
novelty_axes.search_question: Has any prior method specifically targeted critical / super / massive weights during PEFT to improve robustness?
novelty_axes.closest_prior_work_ref: Yu et al. 2024a [similar_prior_work #2] discusses super-weight preservation in quantization but not PEFT robustness
