similar_prior_work.title: Massive Activations in Large Language Models
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Discovered massive activations in hidden states of LLMs, linked them to attention sinks, and showed they reside in a few fixed feature dimensions at specific tokens. Performed zeroing attacks on hidden-state activations.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: This paper shifts from hidden-state activations to the FFN intermediate state and to the underlying W_up/W_gate weights, defining a weight-level attack and a fine-tuning method (MacDrop). The cited paper is properly credited but does much of the conceptual heavy lifting.

similar_prior_work.title: The Super Weight in Large Language Models
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Concurrent work defining 'super weights' — single weights in the FFN that, if zeroed, catastrophically degrade the LLM. Demonstrates that pruning one such weight collapses the model.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Yu et al. define super weight via the W_down projection (intersection of input-side intermediate-state activation and output-side hidden-state activation, picking a single scalar), whereas this paper defines top-k massive weights as rows of W_up and W_gate (output perspective). The conceptual finding overlaps strongly; this paper adds a curriculum-dropout PEFT method (MacDrop) that Yu et al. do not propose, and explicit top-k retaining attacks.

similar_prior_work.title: A Simple and Effective Pruning Approach for Large Language Models (Wanda)
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Activation-aware weight importance scoring for pruning, using a small calibration set to identify weights to keep across every linear layer.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Wanda scores every weight in every linear layer; this paper isolates a tiny subset (~0.0005% of params) localized to a single early layer's W_up/W_gate. Paper claims (without empirical verification) that massive weights would be among Wanda's selected weights — overlap not measured.

similar_prior_work.title: AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for On-Device LLM
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Identifies a small fraction of salient weights (driven by activation magnitudes) that, if quantized carelessly, destroy LLM accuracy.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: AWQ identifies salient weights per linear layer for quantization protection; this paper identifies a single-layer, FFN-specific row set and uses it for analysis and PEFT regularization rather than quantization.

similar_prior_work.title: Regularization of Neural Networks using DropConnect
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Proposes weight-level Bernoulli dropout (drops individual weights rather than activations) as a generalization of standard dropout.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: MacDrop is a DropConnect variant restricted to identified pre-trained massive-weight rows, with a decaying schedule and rollback after each step so that the trained model converges to use the original weights. The targeting and schedule are new but the underlying weight-dropout mechanism is DropConnect. The paper cites Wan et al. only briefly.

similar_prior_work.title: Curriculum Dropout
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Proposes scheduling the dropout probability over training rather than holding it constant, motivated by curriculum-learning principles.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: MacDrop applies the same idea (time-decaying dropout probability) to DropConnect-style weight dropout on identified pre-trained massive weights during PEFT. The schedule (linear/exponential decay) is essentially the curriculum-dropout idea. This prior work is not cited.

potential_missing_citations: Morerio et al., 'Curriculum Dropout', ICCV 2017 — the time-decaying dropout schedule that defines MacDrop's curriculum is the central idea of this earlier paper; the paper should engage with it rather than treating curriculum scheduling as a novel design choice.

Dettmers et al., 'LLM.int8(): 8-bit Matrix Multiplication for Transformers at Scale', NeurIPS 2022 — original observation of outlier feature dimensions in LLMs that destroy quantization, conceptually a precursor to massive activations and outlier-weight phenomena; not cited.

Kovaleva et al., 'BERT Busters: Outlier Dimensions that Disrupt Transformers', Findings of ACL 2021 — early work on small numbers of dimensions whose ablation disproportionately damages transformer performance; conceptually a direct precursor to 'massive weights' phenomenon, not cited.
Puccetti et al., 'Outlier Dimensions that Disrupt Transformers Are Driven by Frequency', EMNLP Findings 2022 — also directly relevant precursor analysis on outlier dimensions, not cited.
parsed.rationale: The paper makes a primarily empirical/analytical contribution that sits atop a well-developed line of work on outlier activations, attention sinks, and salient weights in LLMs. The core observation — that a tiny set of weights in a single early FFN layer dominates LLM functionality — is closely paralleled by concurrent Apple work on 'super weights' (Yu et al. 2024a), which the authors acknowledge but distinguish only by focusing on W_up/W_gate rows rather than W_down scalars; the high-level finding is essentially the same and reduces the conceptual novelty of axes 1 and 2 to 'incremental'. The MacDrop method is a straightforward composition of DropConnect (Wan et al. 2013) with curriculum-scheduled dropout (Morerio et al. 2017, uncited), targeted at the identified massive weights; this is a sensible but minor algorithmic contribution. The genuinely novel pieces are the breadth of the cross-family empirical taxonomy (Mixtral expert collapse at the massive-weight layer, Gemma-2/Phi-3-medium robustness mapped to specific architectural choices, and 405B-scale attack results) and the demonstration that targeted curriculum dropout during PEFT measurably improves robustness to weight-zeroing attacks — though robustness is measured against the authors' own attack and improvements on standard benchmarks are within plausible seed noise. Earlier 'outlier dimension' literature (LLM.int8, BERT Busters) that conceptually anticipates the phenomenon is also not cited. Overall the package is best characterized as an incremental empirical extension of Sun et al. 2024a with a small, derivative methodological add-on; useful as a consolidation but not delivering a clearly new conceptual or methodological advance.

domain_expert.add_to_field: Extends the massive activations / attention sinks line of work by tracing the phenomenon back to specific weights (rows of W_up and W_gate) in a single early FFN layer, and by providing a broader cross-family characterization including Gemma-2 and Phi-3 where the phenomenon is attenuated or absent. Connects this to architectural choices (extra LN layers, residual dropout) that mitigate over-reliance.
domain_expert.beneficiaries: Researchers working on LLM interpretability, attention sinks, KV-cache compression, quantization (which often handles outliers), and architecture design.
domain_expert.importance: The localization to a tiny set of weights in a specific layer is a meaningful refinement of prior activation-level findings, though it builds incrementally on Sun et al. (2024a) and is concurrent with Yu et al. (2024a)'s 'super weight' work.
domain_expert.improvement_meaningful: Conceptually yes — moving from activation-space to weight-space localization opens new analyses. The cross-architecture comparison (Gemma-2, Phi-3) is a valuable contribution by itself.
domain_expert.future_influence: Could influence work on quantization-aware training, pruning baselines (e.g., what Wanda/AWQ are implicitly capturing), and architectural design choices that prevent fragile concentration of function in few weights.

methodology_expert.add_to_field: Introduces a clean operational definition of 'top-k massive weights' tied to an activation-aware probe using only the bos token, plus two complementary attacks (zeroing vs. retaining) that elegantly demonstrate concentration of learned function. MacDrop is a simple, plug-and-play curriculum dropout on identified weights.
methodology_expert.beneficiaries: PEFT practitioners and researchers exploring regularization strategies that respect pre-trained structure; methodology designers studying targeted dropout/dropconnect variants.
methodology_expert.importance: The methodology is principled and reproducible; using a single bos token to identify the critical weights is an elegant simplification. However, MacDrop itself is conceptually simple — a curriculum dropout on a known weight subset — and is not deeply justified beyond intuition.
methodology_expert.improvement_meaningful: Methodologically modest: it is essentially DropConnect with a curated mask and a schedule. The novelty lies more in target selection than in technique.
methodology_expert.future_influence: May inspire more sophisticated regularization or knowledge-preservation schemes during PEFT that explicitly account for critical weight subsets identified via probing.

empirical_evaluation_expert.add_to_field: Provides broad empirical coverage across many model families and sizes (up to 405B), perplexity on three corpora, five zero-shot tasks, LongBench, and a generation benchmark. Includes useful ablations on dropout scope, probability, and curriculum schedule.
empirical_evaluation_expert.beneficiaries: Practitioners selecting fine-tuning recipes and researchers needing reference numbers on how various LLMs respond to weight-space perturbations.
empirical_evaluation_expert.importance: Empirical breadth is a strength. However, downstream gains from MacDrop are small (often 0.3–0.9 points on zero-shot averages, sometimes within noise), no variance/seed analysis is reported, and gains on generation tasks (Table 9) are negligible. Robustness gains under the authors' own zeroing attack are large but somewhat self-fulfilling since MacDrop is designed against that exact threat.
empirical_evaluation_expert.improvement_meaningful: Marginal for clean downstream performance, substantial for robustness against the proposed attack. Without statistical tests or multi-seed runs, the modest clean-task improvements are weak evidence.
empirical_evaluation_expert.future_influence: The empirical maps of massive activations across families and the attack protocol could become useful reference benchmarks; the MacDrop gains alone are unlikely to drive adoption.

skeptical_reviewer.add_to_field: Mostly incremental over Sun et al. (2024a) and concurrent with Yu et al. (2024a). The 'massive weights' concept is essentially the rows producing the already-identified massive activations, so the conceptual leap is limited. The attack scenario (zeroing a known small subset) is somewhat contrived — these models are not normally subject to such weight surgery.
skeptical_reviewer.beneficiaries: Mainly researchers within the narrow massive-activations subfield; broader practical impact is unclear.
skeptical_reviewer.importance: Localizing critical weights is interesting but the framing 'house of cards' overstates real-world fragility, since attackers would need white-box weight access. The MacDrop robustness claim defends against an attack designed by the authors themselves.
skeptical_reviewer.improvement_meaningful: Clean-task improvements are small and likely within seed variance. No confidence intervals, no multi-seed runs, limited datasets per epoch. Long-context improvements are mixed across datasets. Generation tasks show no gain. MacDrop fails on Gemma-2 and Phi-3-medium — exactly the models where the underlying assumption breaks — which is honestly reported but limits applicability.
skeptical_reviewer.future_influence: Likely cited within the niche but unlikely to be transformative; risk that future architectures (already trending toward Gemma-2-style designs) will simply remove the phenomenon, obviating MacDrop.

practical_impact_expert.add_to_field: Offers a low-overhead recipe (~0.35s/step overhead) that can be dropped into existing LoRA/DoRA pipelines. The robustness gains against weight-zeroing are dramatic, and the long-context improvements on some LongBench subsets are useful.
practical_impact_expert.beneficiaries: Engineers fine-tuning Llama-3/Mistral with LoRA/DoRA who want incremental gains and robustness; deployment teams concerned about activation/weight outliers that complicate quantization.
practical_impact_expert.importance: Practical value is modest. For most users seeking better downstream accuracy, gains under one point are unlikely to motivate adoption. However, the diagnostic finding (massive weights exist, can be located cheaply) may inform quantization and pruning practice more than MacDrop itself.
practical_impact_expert.improvement_meaningful: Marginal on standard benchmarks; meaningful for robustness; potentially relevant for quantization-aware design (linking to KVQuant, AWQ).
practical_impact_expert.future_influence: Could influence quantization and outlier-handling approaches more than PEFT practice. MacDrop itself is unlikely to become a default, but the conceptual framework around identifying and treating critical weights may persist.

consensus: The localization of massive activations to specific rows of W_up/W_gate at one early FFN layer is a clean and meaningful refinement of prior work.
The cross-architecture characterization (Gemma-2, Phi-3, Mixtral routing) is empirically valuable.
MacDrop's clean-task improvements are small and often within likely noise; robustness gains against the proposed zeroing attack are large but defend against a self-designed threat.
The work is incremental rather than transformative, building closely on Sun et al. (2024a) and overlapping with concurrent 'super weight' work.
MacDrop does not generalize to architectures without the massive-activation phenomenon, limiting applicability.
disagreement: Domain expert sees real conceptual value in moving from activation- to weight-space, while skeptical reviewer views it as a near-trivial corollary of prior work.
Practical impact expert highlights deployability and quantization implications; skeptical reviewer questions whether the attack scenario is realistic enough to matter.
Empirical evaluation expert sees breadth as a strength; skeptical reviewer emphasizes lack of statistical rigor (no seeds/variance) undermining the modest gains.

definitions_clear: Top-k massive weights are operationally defined as rows of W_up and W_gate at layer l corresponding to the top-k magnitudes of the intermediate state when the bos token is fed.
Two attack types (top-k zeroing and top-k retaining) are clearly defined and contrasted.
MacDrop algorithm is precisely specified in pseudo-PyTorch (Algorithm 1) with explicit mask, rollback, and curriculum schedule.
assumptions_clear: The use of bos token alone as probe is justified by empirical observation across model families.
Layer l is selected per model via post-hoc inspection of intermediate-state magnitudes.
derivations_algorithms_clear: Algorithm 1 is implementable and unambiguous in pseudo-code form.
Curriculum schedules (Step, Epoch(before/after), Exp.) are given explicit formulas in Section 4.3.2.
suggested_improvements_for_formulation: Provide a quantitative threshold or principled criterion for what counts as 'massive' rather than relying on top-k by magnitude.
Specify an algorithmic procedure for identifying layer l (currently post-hoc visual inspection).
Clarify the activation function σ in the FFN decomposition (e.g., SiLU for Llama) for reproducibility.
Discuss whether the dropout in Algorithm 1 is inverted/scaled (mask × W vs mask × W / (1−p)); the unscaled form causes a systematic activation shift not addressed.
Justify the choice to probe with bos only, and analyze whether massive weight indices are stable across other probe tokens / calibration inputs.
Clarify that since W is frozen, dropout affects only the forward signal to adapters; gradient flow implications should be discussed.

supported: Massive activations originate in the FFN intermediate state of an early layer (well-supported by Figure 3 and Appendix D across many model families).
Top-k zeroing severely degrades LLM performance while top-k retaining causes far less damage on Llama-3 family (Table 1, Figure 4).
Mixtral router becomes nearly deterministic to one expert at the massive-weight layer for the bos token (Figure 5).
MacDrop substantially improves robustness against the authors' top-k zeroing attack (Table 4).
MacDrop provides no/negative gains for Gemma-2 and Phi-3-medium families (Appendix F Table 8) — limitation transparently reported.
partially_supported: 'bos token always triggers massive activations' — supported with caveats; Appendix C shows exceptions (e.g., Gemma-2-27b only when bos is at first position; Llama-2/Phi-3 do not respond to bos in middle/end positions).
MacDrop 'generally improves' zero-shot downstream performance — gains are small (typically <1 point), only on two models in main text; broader Appendix F shows mixed/neutral results.
MacDrop improves long-context performance — aggregate LongBench improvements are modest with per-task regressions; single seed, no significance testing (Table 2).
Ablation-based recommendation of step/exp curriculum with moderate p_0 — differences in Table 5 are within ~0.5 points, plausibly within seed noise, and tested only on Llama-3-8B.
unsupported_or_overstated: Massive weights are 'predominantly learned during pre-training' — only post-hoc attack asymmetry is shown; no training-dynamics evidence (gradients, weight trajectories) is provided.
Pre-training dataset does not significantly influence emergence — based on comparison across Llama versions sharing much architecture/recipe; confounded.
Architectural attribution of robustness (extra LN in Gemma-2, residual dropout in Phi-3-medium) — plausible but not causally tested via controlled retraining.
Claim of distinction from Wanda/AWQ-selected weights — no empirical overlap analysis is provided.
Abstract's strong framing 'MacDrop generally improves performance' is not consistent with negligible generation-task gains (Appendix G) and flat/negative results on several model families.
suggested_improvements_for_claim_support: Add an experiment comparing massive-weight-row dropout to dropout on equally-sized random row subsets and on Wanda/AWQ-selected weights to isolate specificity.
Report multiple seeds with confidence intervals or significance tests for all MacDrop comparisons.

Test robustness against other attack types (random perturbations, quantization noise, adversarial weight perturbations) to demonstrate that MacDrop's robustness gains are not specific to the authors' self-defined attack.
Provide training-dynamics evidence (e.g., gradient norm or weight magnitude trajectories during pre-training of a small model) to substantiate the 'dominantly learned during pre-training' claim.
Empirically measure overlap between massive weights and Wanda/AWQ-selected weights.
Analyze stability of massive-weight indices across probe inputs beyond bos token.
Conduct a controlled experiment retraining a small Llama-like model with extra LN or residual dropout to causally test the architectural attribution.

experimental_rigor.baseline_quality: Baselines are limited to vanilla LoRA and DoRA without MacDrop. There is no comparison to closely related alternatives (e.g., random-row dropout, LoRA-internal dropout variants, weight-decay on massive rows, or Wanda/AWQ-aware regularization).
missing_baselines: Random row dropout matched in count to massive weights
Standard LoRA dropout / weight decay tuned variants
Comparison with concurrent 'super weight' (Yu et al., 2024a) approach
Full fine-tuning with vs without MacDrop
experimental_rigor.dataset_quality: Fine-tuning uses only alpaca_gpt4_en; evaluation uses standard benchmarks (five common-sense zero-shot tasks, LongBench, Spec-Bench). Reasonable but narrow for the breadth of claims.
better_datasets: Multiple instruction-tuning datasets to demonstrate generality
Domain-specific fine-tuning (math, code) to test transfer
experimental_rigor.metric_appropriateness: Perplexity, zero-shot accuracy, and LongBench metrics are appropriate. Use of Prometheus-2-7B for generation evaluation is reasonable but limited.
better_metric: Pairwise comparison or human evaluation for generation tasks
Attack-strength sweeps (k vs performance) for robustness claims
missing_ablations: Sensitivity of MacDrop to k (number of massive weights dropped)
Random-row dropout control matched in element count
Effect on full fine-tuning vs PEFT
Stability of massive weight indices after fine-tuning (with/without MacDrop)
Effect of total training steps T on the curriculum
experimental_rigor.statistical_significance: No seeds, error bars, or significance tests are reported. Many reported gains (0.4–0.9 points) are within plausible seed noise.
better_significance_test_or_confidence_i: Paired bootstrap or paired t-tests across tasks
experimental_rigor.hyperparameter_clarity: PEFT hyperparameters are listed in Appendix Table 6. MacDrop hyperparameters (k=5, p_0=0.8) are stated but selection rationale and sensitivity are not fully analyzed for all models.
possible_confounders: Single seed runs leave seed variance as a major confounder for small gains.
Llama-3.1-405B uses 8-bit quantization which may affect activation magnitudes and attack sensitivity.
Mixtral attack restricted to a single expert (expert 4) based on bos routing; may not reflect real-input behavior.
Layer l selection is post-hoc visual inspection, with no cross-validation across probe inputs.
Llama version comparisons across data-cutoffs share architecture/recipe, conflating data and model effects.
Use of bos-only probing may bias which dimensions/rows are deemed 'massive' relative to typical content tokens.
confounder_reduction_alternatives: Use multiple seeds across all benchmarks
Repeat key Llama-3-405B attacks at full precision (or compare bit-widths)
Probe massive weights using diverse calibration inputs rather than bos only
Controlled training experiments isolating architectural choices (extra LN, residual dropout)
Analyze Mixtral attacks under realistic multi-token inputs and across all activated experts
suggestions_for_improving_reproducibilit: Release code for identifying massive weights and applying MacDrop.
Provide exact seeds and full hyperparameter sweeps for ablations.
Specify the activation function in the FFN decomposition.
Document layer-selection procedure as an algorithm rather than visual inspection.
Report variance over multiple seeds for all reported numbers.

strengths: Clean and reproducible localization of massive activations to specific rows of W_up and W_gate at a single early FFN layer, providing a useful refinement over prior activation-level findings (Sun et al., 2024a).

Broad empirical coverage across many LLM families (Llama-2/3/3.1 up to 405B, Mistral, Mixtral, Phi-3, Gemma-2) and model sizes, including a useful negative characterization showing which architectures (Gemma-2, Phi-3-medium) avoid the phenomenon.
Two complementary attacks (top-k zeroing vs. top-k retaining) provide striking and well-presented evidence that ~0.0005% of parameters dominate model functionality in vulnerable families.
Operational simplification of identifying critical weights via the bos token alone — elegant and cheap, with thorough Appendix C documentation of caveats per family.
MacDrop is a simple, plug-and-play method with low overhead (~0.35s/step) and clear pseudo-code (Algorithm 1).
Cross-architecture analysis links robustness to architectural choices (extra LN in Gemma-2, residual dropout in Phi-3-medium), providing actionable design insight.
Honest reporting of limitations: authors transparently show MacDrop fails on Gemma-2 and Phi-3-medium and that generation-task gains are limited.
weaknesses: Conceptual novelty over Sun et al. (2024a) and concurrent Yu et al. (2024a) 'super weight' work is incremental — defining 'massive weights' as the rows producing already-identified massive activations is a relatively small step.

MacDrop's clean-task improvements are small (typically 0.3–0.9 points on zero-shot averages) and reported with no seeds, error bars, or significance tests, making it difficult to distinguish gains from noise.

Robustness gains in Table 4 defend against an attack designed by the authors themselves; no evaluation against other perturbation types (random weight noise, quantization noise, adversarial perturbations) is provided.

Critical missing baseline: dropout on a random row subset matched in element count to massive weights, which would isolate whether the specificity of the target matters versus just additional regularization.
No empirical comparison to closely related weight-importance methods (Wanda, AWQ) despite the Appendix H discussion claiming a relationship.
Claim that massive weights are 'predominantly learned during pre-training' is interpretive: only post-hoc attack asymmetry is shown, with no training-dynamics or gradient-trajectory evidence.
Architectural attribution of robustness to extra LN / residual dropout is plausible but not causally tested via controlled retraining.
Layer l selection is via post-hoc visual inspection rather than an algorithmic procedure; the procedure for probing with bos only is not validated against alternative calibration inputs.
Long-context (LongBench) and generation (Spec-Bench) gains are mixed at the subtask level and aggregate gains are small; abstract framing of 'generally improves performance' is somewhat overstated.

The 'house of cards' threat model assumes white-box weight access and adversary ability to surgically zero weights, which is non-standard and limits the practical urgency of the robustness contribution.
minor_issues: The dropout operation in Algorithm 1 appears unscaled (mask * W rather than mask * W / (1-p)); the resulting systematic activation-shift during training is not discussed.
Activation function σ in the FFN decomposition is unspecified (SiLU for Llama family); should be made explicit for reproducibility.
Llama-3.1-405B is evaluated in 8-bit, which may interact with activation magnitudes and attack sensitivity; results should be flagged with this caveat.
Mixtral attacks restrict to expert 4 based on bos routing only; behavior under realistic multi-token inputs is not analyzed.
Ablation Table 5 differences (75.5–76.5) are within likely seed noise, yet specific curriculum recommendations are made.
Claim that pre-training data does not influence massive phenomena (based on cross-Llama-version comparison) is confounded by shared architecture and recipe.
Sensitivity of MacDrop to k (the number of dropped massive weight rows) is not ablated.
Stability of identified massive-weight indices after fine-tuning (with or without MacDrop) is not reported.
questions_for_authors: How does MacDrop compare against a control where dropout is applied to a random row subset (of equal size) in W_up / W_gate at the same layer l? This is essential to establish target specificity.
Can you report multi-seed runs with confidence intervals or paired significance tests for the main downstream and LongBench tables? Many gains appear within plausible noise.
Does MacDrop improve robustness against attacks other than your own top-k zeroing (e.g., random Gaussian weight perturbations, quantization to low bit-widths, or adversarial weight perturbations)?
What is the empirical overlap between top-k massive weight rows and weights selected by Wanda / AWQ in the same projections?
Is the dropout applied with inverted scaling (mask / (1-p))? If not, how do you address the activation-shift this introduces during training?
How are massive weight indices affected if probing uses calibration inputs other than the bos token alone (e.g., a small text corpus)?

What evidence supports the claim that massive weights are 'predominantly learned during pre-training'? Have you examined the trajectory of these weights' magnitudes during a smaller-scale pre-training run?
How is layer l selected algorithmically? Is it robust across model checkpoints (pre-trained vs instruction-tuned)?
Why are the Mixtral attack results restricted to expert 4? Do results change when content tokens (not just bos) are used for routing analysis?

Can you provide a controlled experiment (e.g., re-training a small Llama-like model with extra LN or residual dropout) to causally test the architectural attribution for Gemma-2 / Phi-3-medium robustness?
parsed.recommendation_rationale: The paper makes a clean and well-documented empirical contribution by tracing massive activations to specific FFN weights and providing thorough cross-architecture characterization, including informative negative cases. The two-attack design and the bos-token probe are elegant. However, the conceptual leap over Sun et al. (2024a) and concurrent 'super weight' work is incremental, and the MacDrop method — the main algorithmic contribution — yields only small clean-task improvements reported without statistical rigor (no seeds, no significance tests). Its most dramatic results (robustness gains) defend against a self-designed attack, and a critical baseline (random-row dropout matched in count) is missing. Limitations (no gains on Gemma-2 / Phi-3-medium / generation tasks) are reported honestly but constrain applicability. The work is solid but not transformative; with stronger statistical reporting, additional baselines, and broader robustness evaluation, it could clearly cross the acceptance bar.

parsed.summary_of_paper:
The paper extends prior work on massive activations and attention sinks in LLMs by tracing their origin to the intermediate state of an early-layer feed-forward network (FFN). The authors define 'top-k massive weights' as the rows of W_up and W_gate at this specific layer whose outputs produce the top-k magnitudes of the intermediate state when probed with the bos token. They show via two opposing attacks (top-k zeroing vs. top-k retaining) that this tiny set of weights (~0.0005% of parameters in Llama-3-8B) dominates model functionality in Llama-2/3/3.1, Mistral, Mixtral, and Phi-3-mini families, while Gemma-2 (extra LN) and Phi-3-medium (residual dropout) are robust. Building on this, they propose MacDrop, a curriculum dropout applied to pre-trained massive-weight rows during LoRA/DoRA fine-tuning, with the dropout probability decaying from a high initial value to zero by the end of training.

MacDrop is evaluated on zero-shot tasks, LongBench, and Spec-Bench generation across multiple model families. It yields modest improvements on zero-shot tasks (typically 0.3–0.9 points on the average), mixed improvements on LongBench, and large robustness gains when the resulting fine-tuned models are subjected to the authors' own top-k zeroing attack. The method does not help families that lack the massive-activation phenomenon (Gemma-2, Phi-3-medium), which the authors transparently report.
main_strengths: Clean and reproducible localization of massive activations to specific rows of W_up and W_gate at a single early FFN layer, refining the activation-level findings of Sun et al. (2024a).
Broad empirical coverage across many LLM families (Llama-2/3/3.1 up to 405B, Mistral, Mixtral, Phi-3, Gemma-2), including informative negative cases where the phenomenon does not occur.
Two complementary attacks (top-k zeroing vs. top-k retaining) provide striking, well-presented evidence that ~0.0005% of parameters dominate functionality in vulnerable families.
Operationally elegant bos-token probe for identifying critical weights, with thorough Appendix C documentation of per-family caveats.
MacDrop is simple, plug-and-play, with low overhead (~0.35s/step) and clear pseudo-code; ablations cover scope, probability, and curriculum schedule.
Architectural attribution (extra LN in Gemma-2, residual dropout in Phi-3-medium) provides actionable design insight, even if not causally tested.
Honest reporting of limitations: MacDrop fails on Gemma-2 and Phi-3-medium and shows only marginal gains on generation tasks; the authors do not overclaim.
main_weaknesses: Conceptual novelty over Sun et al. (2024a) and concurrent Yu et al. (2024a) 'super weight' work is incremental; defining massive weights as rows producing already-identified massive activations is a relatively small step.

MacDrop's clean-task improvements are small (typically 0.3–0.9 points on zero-shot averages) and reported with no multi-seed runs, error bars, or significance tests, making it hard to distinguish gains from noise.

The most dramatic results (robustness in Table 4) defend specifically against the authors' own attack; no other perturbation types (random noise, quantization, adversarial weight perturbations) are evaluated.

A critical baseline is missing: dropout on a random row subset of W_up/W_gate matched in element count to massive weights. Without this, target specificity cannot be distinguished from additional regularization.
No empirical comparison to related weight-importance methods (Wanda, AWQ), despite Appendix H discussing their conceptual relationship.
The claim that massive weights are 'predominantly learned during pre-training' is interpretive: only post-hoc attack asymmetry is shown, with no training-dynamics or gradient-trajectory evidence.
Architectural attribution of robustness (extra LN, residual dropout) is plausible but not causally tested via controlled training experiments.
Layer l and Mixtral expert selection are based on post-hoc visual inspection and bos-token routing only; alternative calibration procedures are not validated.
Aggregate LongBench gains are small and per-subtask results are mixed; the abstract framing of 'generally improves performance' is somewhat overstated.
The 'house of cards' threat model assumes white-box weight access plus surgical zeroing, which is non-standard and limits the practical urgency of the robustness contribution.
detailed_comments: Section 2.2 / Figure 3: The decomposition tracing massive activations to FFN(LN(ĥ_2)) → h_2 is convincing for Llama-3-8B, and Appendix D extends this nicely across families. However, the procedure for selecting the specific layer l is implicit (visual inspection of the top-3 magnitudes). An algorithmic criterion (e.g., layer with maximum intermediate-state max/median ratio above a threshold) would make the work more reproducible.

Section 2.3 / Table 1: The contrast between top-5 zeroing and top-5 retaining is the paper's strongest empirical result and is well-framed. It would be even more compelling with a 'random-5 retaining' baseline (keep 5 random rows, zero the rest) to confirm that the 5 specific rows, rather than any 5 rows, are sufficient.

Section 2.3 / Figure 4: The robustness curves across families are very informative. Adding a control where the same number of weights are zeroed at non-massive layers would strengthen the claim that the layer-specific localization is what matters.

Section 3 / Algorithm 1: It is unclear whether the dropout uses inverted scaling (multiplying surviving weights by 1/(1-p)). As written, the masking introduces a systematic shift in the intermediate-state magnitudes during training, which is then removed at inference. This activation-shift effect deserves discussion and possibly an inverted-scaling ablation.

Section 4.1 / Tables 3 and 4: The clean improvements are small (within plausible seed noise) while the under-attack robustness gains are large. Because the attack is the very phenomenon MacDrop is designed to mitigate, this is partially circular. Robustness to other perturbation types (Gaussian weight noise of matched norm, 4-bit quantization, gradient-based adversarial perturbations) would meaningfully strengthen the case.

Section 4.2 / Table 2: LongBench gains are uneven across subtasks (e.g., big improvements on TriviaQA and Musique, but drops on NrtvQA and PCount). It would be useful to discuss why MacDrop helps disproportionately on certain task types.

Section 4.3 / Table 5: Differences across curriculum schedules are within ~1 point and likely within seed noise, yet specific recommendations (Step or Exp with small α from moderately high p_0) are made. These recommendations should be supported by multi-seed experiments or a statistical test.

Appendix F / Table 8: The honest reporting that MacDrop does not help Gemma-2 / Phi-3-medium is appreciated, but it raises the question of whether MacDrop is best understood as a fix for a specific family of architectures rather than a general PEFT improvement.
Appendix G: The Spec-Bench results (Table 9) show essentially flat performance with MacDrop. This should be reflected more clearly in the abstract/intro framing.

Appendix H: The discussion of the relationship to Wanda, AWQ, and 'super weight' is helpful, but a quantitative overlap analysis (do top-k massive weight rows fall within the top-X% of Wanda or AWQ importance scores?) would situate the contribution more concretely.
questions_for_authors: How does MacDrop compare against a control where dropout is applied to a random row subset of equal size in W_up / W_gate at the same layer l? This is essential to isolate target specificity.
Can you report multi-seed runs with confidence intervals or paired significance tests for Tables 2, 3, 4, and 5? Many gains appear within plausible seed noise.
Does MacDrop improve robustness against attacks other than top-k zeroing (e.g., random Gaussian weight perturbations of matched norm, low-bit quantization, adversarial weight perturbations)?
What is the empirical overlap between top-k massive weight rows and weights selected by Wanda or AWQ in the same projection matrices?
Is dropout applied with inverted scaling (mask / (1-p))? If not, how do you address the activation-magnitude shift this introduces during training versus inference?
How are the identified massive-weight indices affected if probing uses calibration inputs other than the bos token alone (e.g., a small natural-text corpus or multiple delimiter tokens)?

What evidence supports the claim that massive weights are 'predominantly learned during pre-training'? Have you traced the magnitude trajectory of these weights during a smaller-scale pre-training run?
How is layer l selected algorithmically? Is the selection stable across pre-trained vs instruction-tuned checkpoints of the same model?
Why are the Mixtral attacks restricted to expert 4? Do results change when routing is analyzed using diverse content tokens rather than just bos?

Can you provide a controlled experiment (e.g., re-training a small Llama-like model with extra LN or residual dropout) to causally test the architectural attribution for Gemma-2 / Phi-3-medium robustness?
After fine-tuning (with and without MacDrop), do the identified massive-weight indices remain the same, or shift? This would help interpret what MacDrop is doing mechanistically.
suggestions_for_improvement: Add a random-row-dropout baseline matched in element count to massive weights to establish target specificity; this is the single most important experiment to add.
Report multi-seed averages with standard deviations or 95% CIs for all main tables (2, 3, 4, 5); add paired significance tests for comparisons claiming improvement.

Evaluate MacDrop's robustness against non-zeroing perturbations (Gaussian noise, low-bit quantization, adversarial weight perturbations) to demonstrate that gains generalize beyond the self-designed attack.
Add a quantitative comparison/overlap analysis with Wanda and AWQ on the same projection matrices.
Clarify the dropout scaling convention in Algorithm 1 and discuss the activation-magnitude shift; consider an inverted-scaling ablation.
Provide an algorithmic procedure for selecting layer l rather than visual inspection, and validate that the bos-only probe agrees with multi-token calibration probes.
Soften the abstract/intro framing about 'generally improves performance' to reflect that improvements are modest, mixed on LongBench/Spec-Bench, and absent on Gemma-2 / Phi-3-medium.
Consider a controlled small-scale retraining experiment to causally test whether adding LN or residual dropout suppresses massive-weight formation, supporting the architectural attribution.
Add an ablation over k (the number of dropped massive-weight rows) and over the choice of probing input.
Move the threat-model framing ('house of cards') into context: discuss when white-box weight-level attacks are realistic, and contrast with practical settings.
parsed.overall_recommendation: borderline (lean reject without revisions; lean accept with the requested baselines and statistical reporting)

misrepresentations: The detailed comment on Algorithm 1 states that 'the masking introduces a systematic shift in the intermediate-state magnitudes during training, which is then removed at inference.' This is approximately correct but should be phrased more carefully: standard weight-dropout (DropConnect) typically does not use inverted scaling, so this is a known design choice rather than necessarily a flaw. The framing should be a question/clarification, not a criticism.
missed_strengths: The paper provides extensive appendix material (Appendices C, D, E) with per-family visualizations that substantially aid reproducibility and understanding.
The connection between massive weights and Mixtral's expert routing (Figure 5, showing one expert dominates at the massive-weights layer) is a novel and interesting finding.
missed_weaknesses: The paper's central conceptual claim — that massive weights are 'rows of W_up and W_gate' — is partly tautological: rows of these matrices by definition determine the output dimensions of the intermediate state, so identifying 'rows producing top-k magnitudes' is essentially identifying the top-k output dimensions, which is what Sun et al. (2024a) already characterized.
Algorithm 1 only drops weights in a single layer l; this is a very narrow form of regularization and the paper does not explore whether dropping at multiple candidate layers would behave differently.
too_strong_claims: The overall recommendation phrasing 'lean reject without revisions; lean accept with the requested baselines' is somewhat hedged — the score card (overall 5) is consistent with borderline, which is acceptable.
The claim in detailed comments that small clean-task gains are 'within plausible seed noise' is an inference, not verified; should be phrased as a concern rather than a fact.
corrections_needed: Soften the Algorithm 1 critique to a clarification question about scaling convention rather than asserting it is a flaw; note that weight-level dropout (DropConnect) often does not use inverted scaling.
Reframe 'within plausible seed noise' claims as concerns/questions rather than assertions, since no seed variance is reported either way.
Add a strength acknowledging the Mixtral expert-routing finding (Figure 5) as a novel observation.
Add a weakness noting the partly tautological nature of identifying 'rows producing top-k output magnitudes' given prior characterization of massive activation dimensions.
Consider noting that MacDrop is restricted to a single layer and multi-layer variants are not explored.

Novel observation (Figure 5) that in Mixtral's MoE architecture, the layer containing massive weights exhibits highly skewed router probabilities concentrating on a single expert — an interesting connection between massive activations and expert routing.
Operationally simple bos-token probe for identifying critical weights, with thorough Appendix C documentation of per-family caveats and Appendix D per-family visualizations that aid reproducibility.
MacDrop is simple, plug-and-play, with low overhead (~0.35s/step) and clear pseudo-code; ablations cover scope, probability, and curriculum schedule.

main_weaknesses: Conceptual novelty over Sun et al. (2024a) and concurrent Yu et al. (2024a) 'super weight' work is incremental. Moreover, the definition of massive weights as 'rows of W_up and W_gate producing the top-k output magnitudes' is partly tautological given prior characterization of massive-activation feature dimensions: the row index is, by construction, the same as the feature-dimension index identified by prior work.

MacDrop's clean-task improvements are small (typically 0.3–0.9 points on zero-shot averages) and reported with no multi-seed runs, error bars, or significance tests, making it difficult to assess whether gains are statistically meaningful.

Layer l and Mixtral expert selection rely on post-hoc visual inspection of magnitude plots and bos-token routing only; an algorithmic selection criterion and alternative calibration procedures are not validated.
MacDrop is restricted to dropping weights in a single layer l; multi-layer variants or sensitivity to the layer choice are not explored.
